content:
Most developers don’t become better simply by writing more code. They improve because they learn which habits quietly create long-term problems, and they stop repeating them.

8 min read
1 hour ago

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

8 Things Senior Engineers Avoid in Every ProjectWhen I first started building production software, I assumed that becoming a better engineer meant learning more languages, mastering new frameworks, and writing code faster. Every completed feature felt like progress, and every pull request gave me confidence that I was improving. Looking back, I wasn’t entirely wrong, but I was focusing on only one side of software engineering.
The engineers I admired most approached projects very differently. They weren’t obsessed with writing clever solutions or finishing tasks before everyone else. Instead, they seemed unusually good at avoiding problems that the rest of us accidentally created. Their code reviews contained fewer surprises, their features required fewer emergency fixes, and when requirements inevitably changed, their code adapted with far less effort.
It took several years of maintaining production systems before I understood why. Software projects rarely become difficult because someone forgot a language feature or chose the wrong syntax. They become difficult because…
